paper_title: The Lies Characters Tell: Utilizing Large Language Models to Normalize Adversarial Unicode Perturbations

ground_truth.abstract:
text[[144, 290, 460, 672]]
Homoglyphs, Unicode characters that are visually homogeneous to Latin letters, are widely used to mask offensive content. Dynamic strategies are needed to combat homoglyphs as the Unicode library is ever- expanding and new substitution possibilities for Latin letters continuously emerge. The present study investigated two novel mitigation approaches that do not rely on strict mappings but instead harness the power of large language models to neutralize both known and unknown homoglyphs: (1) indirectly normalizing homoglyphs by replacing non- Latin characters with a delimiter and prompting large language models to "fill in the blanks" and (2) directly normalizing homoglyphs by using large language models to determine which characters should be replaced with Latin letters. We found that GPT- 4o- mini constructed normalized text with an average cosine similarity score of 0.91 to the original tweets when applying our indirect method and 0.96 to the original tweets when applying our direct method. This study indicates that large language model- based normalization techniques can effectively unmask offensive content concealed by homoglyphs. Code and data are available in our GitHub repository.

sub_title[[118, 686, 257, 701]]
ground_truth.introduction_last_third:
first to utilize large language models to normalize homoglyphs.- Comparing the robustness of transformer-based hate speech detection models on homoglyph-infused and normalized text.

sub_title[[117, 84, 267, 99]]
ground_truth.conclusion:
text[[510, 792, 881, 920]]
The homoglyph normalization tools of the future should not rely on rigid mappings or statically learned data. As expected, hate speech detection models performed best on human normalized text, but normalization of this type is untenable for many applications. We show that by harnessing the power of large language models, robust indirect and direct techniques for combating homoglyphs are possible.

sub_title[[117, 84, 248, 99]]

original_summary: First, the study introduces two novel large language model (LLM)-based approaches for homoglyph normalization—indirect normalization via delimiter-based blank filling and direct normalization through LLM-driven character substitution—that eliminate reliance on static Unicode mappings. Through these methods, GPT-4o-mini achieved high fidelity in reconstructing original text, with cosine similarity scores of 0.91 and 0.96, respectively, demonstrating superior generalization to unseen homoglyph variants. Additionally, the authors constructed a novel dataset of 29,846 homoglyph-augmented tweets derived from real-world content and evaluated the robustness of hate speech detection models, revealing that LLM-normalized text significantly restores detection performance compared to unnormalized homoglyph inputs. Finally, the work establishes that LLM-driven normalization is a scalable and effective alternative to manual or rule-based methods for combating evolving homoglyph-based obfuscation.

missing_points: The study indicates that large language model-based normalization techniques can effectively unmask offensive content concealed by homoglyphs.
unsupported_claims: The authors constructed a novel dataset of 29,846 homoglyph-augmented tweets derived from real-world content.
LLM-normalized text significantly restores detection performance compared to unnormalized homoglyph inputs.
original_validation.critique: The summary should remove the claim about constructing a novel dataset of 29,846 homoglyph-augmented tweets as this is not mentioned in the original text. Additionally, the claim about LLM-normalized text significantly restoring detection performance is not supported by the provided evidence. The summary should include the point that large language model-based normalization techniques can effectively unmask offensive content concealed by homoglyphs, as highlighted in the original text.

final_summary: First, the study introduces two novel large language model (LLM)-based approaches for homoglyph normalization—indirect normalization via delimiter-based blank filling and direct normalization through LLM-driven character substitution—that eliminate reliance on static Unicode mappings. Through these methods, GPT-4o-mini achieved high fidelity in reconstructing original text, with cosine similarity scores of 0.91 and 0.96, respectively, demonstrating superior generalization to unseen homoglyph variants. The study indicates that large language model-based normalization techniques can effectively unmask offensive content concealed by homoglyphs. Finally, the work establishes that LLM-driven normalization is a scalable and effective alternative to manual or rule-based methods for combating evolving homoglyph-based obfuscation.

final_validation.critique: The summary accurately reflects the core contributions and findings of the original text. It correctly identifies the two novel approaches for homoglyph normalization, the effectiveness of these methods as demonstrated by the cosine similarity scores, and the scalability and effectiveness of LLM-driven normalization techniques. No major points are omitted, and there are no unsupported claims.
